workout.title: Chest Day - BODYBUILD
workout.description: Chest Day workout focusing on bodybuild training.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a pair of dumbbells nearby.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a pair of dumbbells nearby.
Sit on the bench with the dumbbells resting on your thighs, feet flat on the floor for stability.
Use your legs to help lift the dumbbells as you lie back so the weights are beside your upper chest; hold them with a neutral or slightly supinated grip.
Retract your shoulder blades and press your upper back into the bench so your chest is proud (maintain a stable supine position on the incline).
Plant your feet and brace your core to avoid excessive arching of the spine.
Press the dumbbells upward and slightly together in a controlled path until your arms are almost fully extended (do not lock the elbows).
Exhale as you press up, pause briefly at the top with control, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start position at the upper chest level.
Keep the elbows at a moderate angle (not flared excessively) and the wrists neutral throughout the movement.
Repeat for desired repetitions and use a spotter for heavy sets or if you cannot safely return the weights to your thighs/stand them up.
exercises.description: The dumbbell incline bench press is performed lying supine on an incline bench and pressing dumbbells upward to emphasize the upper portion of the chest. It builds strength and mass in the upper pectorals while also recruiting the front delts and triceps.

exercises.name: Cable Bent Over Single Arm Crossover
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.

Stand to the side of the pulley with a staggered or shoulder-width stance and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly 30–45° from vertical (slight bend in knees); maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.
Grab the handle with the hand farthest from the machine, arm extended out to the side and palm facing down or slightly inward; allow a soft bend in the elbow.

Keep your shoulder down and stable, then exhale and pull the handle in a horizontal arc across your body toward the center of your chest, leading with the elbow and squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Pause briefly with the chest contracted, keeping the torso still (avoid rotating the ribs or shoulders), then inhale and slowly reverse the motion to return the hand to the start under control.
Perform the desired reps, then switch sides and repeat. Maintain wrist neutrality and controlled tempo throughout.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable movement performed from a bent-over stance that sweeps the working arm across the body to load and compress the chest fibers, emphasizing the inner and upper pectoral region while the torso is stabilized.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

workout.title: Chest Day - BODYBUILD
workout.description: Chest Day workout focusing on bodybuild training.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench with a dumbbell resting on each thigh.
Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Use your legs and core to help bring the dumbbells up to chest height so your upper arms are about 45 degrees from your torso and palms are facing forward (or slightly inward if preferred).

Inhale, then slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner until your elbows are roughly level with the bench or just below chest level, keeping wrists neutral and elbows tracking slightly tucked.

Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulder blade position, then exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are almost fully extended — avoid locking the elbows.
Control the descent back to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
To finish the set, bring the dumbbells safely to your knees, sit up, and set them down or have a spotter assist if using heavy weight.
exercises.description: The dumbbell bench press is a supine pressing movement performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and delts for elbow and shoulder extension. Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion and require more stabilization from the forearms and serratus anterior.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Chest Press
exercises.notes: Set the pulleys to chest height and attach single-handle grips to each side.
instructions: Set the pulleys to chest height and attach single-handle grips to each side.
Stand facing away from the machine and grab a handle in each hand with a neutral or palms-down grip; bring the handles to chest level with elbows bent and hands near the sternum.
Step forward into a staggered or shoulder-width stance so one foot is slightly in front of the other; brace your core and keep a slight bend in the knees.
Keep shoulders down and back, chest up, and press both handles forward and inward until your arms are almost fully extended (do not lock the elbows).
Exhale as you press; hold the end position briefly while keeping tension on the cables.
Inhale and slowly control the return of the handles back to the starting position at chest level, allowing the shoulder blades to set but not excessively retract.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining a stable torso and avoiding leaning forward or using excessive lower-back arching.
exercises.description: A standing cable press that targets the chest by pressing handles forward from chest level against cable resistance; the movement also engages the anterior delts and triceps while the core stabilizes the torso. Performed facing away from the cable column with a staggered stance for balance.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Fly
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and lower back in a neutral position.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and lower back in a neutral position.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral or slightly pronated grip and extend the arms straight above your chest, palms facing each other.
Keep a slight, fixed bend in the elbows throughout the movement (do not lock the elbows).
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, maintaining the slight elbow bend until you feel a stretch in the chest or your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor.
Keep the shoulder blades stable on the bench (no excessive shrugging) and avoid letting the dumbbells travel behind the plane of your torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom under control, then exhale and bring the dumbbells back up along the same arc, focusing on squeezing the pectorals until the weights meet above the sternum.
Perform each rep with controlled tempo and avoid bouncing or using momentum; select a weight that allows full control and a safe range of motion.
exercises.description: A supine resistance exercise performed with dumbbells that isolates the chest by moving the arms in a wide arc from above the sternum to the sides, producing a deep stretch and concentric squeeze of the pectorals.

exercises.name: Lever Pec Deck Fly
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat so the handles are roughly at mid-chest level when seated and your back rests flat against the pad.
instructions: Adjust the seat so the handles are roughly at mid-chest level when seated and your back rests flat against the pad.
Select an appropriate weight and sit upright with feet flat on the floor, chest up and shoulders down and back.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or slightly pronated grip and position your upper arms roughly parallel to the floor with a small bend in the elbows.
Start with the handles out to the sides so your chest is stretched but shoulders are not overstretched; maintain a stable torso against the back pad.
Exhale and drive the handles together in a smooth horizontal arc by contracting the pectorals, bringing your hands close together or slightly in front of the chest.
Pause briefly at peak contraction, squeezing the chest while keeping elbows slightly bent and avoiding shoulder shrugging.
Inhale and slowly reverse the movement under control, allowing the arms to return to the start position until you feel a controlled stretch in the chest.
Repeat for desired repetitions, keeping tempo controlled and avoiding leaning forward or using momentum to move the weight.
exercises.description: Seated machine chest fly that brings the arms together in a horizontal arc to isolate and contract the pectoral muscles. Performed on a lever/pec-deck machine with the chest supported and elbows slightly bent throughout the movement.

exercises.name: Cable Standing-Up Straight Crossovers
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to a high position and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to a high position and select an appropriate weight.
Stand upright between the towers with feet hip-width or in a slight staggered stance for balance, facing forward (no forward bend).
Grasp a handle in each hand with palms facing down or slightly inward; start with arms out to the sides at shoulder height and a slight bend in the elbows.
Engage your core, draw shoulder blades down and back, and maintain a neutral spine throughout the movement.
Exhale and pull both handles together in a wide arc in front of your body, meeting or crossing hands at chest level while squeezing the pectorals.
Pause briefly at peak contraction, keeping elbows softly bent and shoulders stable (avoid shrugging).
Inhale and slowly reverse the motion, allowing the cables to return your arms to the start position under control.
Repeat for desired repetitions, maintaining upright posture and controlled tempo; adjust weight if form breaks down.
exercises.description: Standing cable crossovers performed from high pulleys while remaining upright; the movement brings the handles together in front of the chest to horizontally adduct the arms and contract the pectorals. Keep a slight bend in the elbows and perform a controlled squeeze at the midline.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
Sit with your back and head firmly against the bench, feet flat on the floor, and brace your core (tighten abs and glutes) to maintain a stable, slight arch in the lower back.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar directly above your upper chest/shoulder line; have a spotter if needed.
Inhale, bend the elbows, and lower the bar under control to the upper chest/clavicle area (touch point just below the top of the sternum) keeping elbows at roughly 30–60° from the torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom without bouncing, ensuring the forearms remain vertical and the core stays braced.
Exhale and press the bar back up in a slightly backward arc until the arms are fully extended but not locked aggressively at the elbows.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, then carefully re-rack the bar on the uprights with control or have the spotter assist.
exercises.description: The barbell incline bench press is a compound pressing movement performed on an inclined bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the pectorals while also engaging the anterior delts and triceps. It is done from a supine position on an incline bench, pressing a barbell vertically from chest to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45° and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45° and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Lie back so your upper back and head are supported (supine on the incline bench) and plant your feet flat on the floor for stability.
Bring the dumbbells up to shoulder level with palms facing forward and elbows bent (upper arms roughly 45° from the torso) while retracting the shoulder blades slightly.
Unrack the dumbbells by pressing them up and then lowering them to start position so the handles are beside your upper chest/shoulder area.
Press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are nearly straight (avoid locking or hyperextending the elbows); exhale during the press.
Pause briefly at the top with tension in the pectorals, then slowly lower the dumbbells under control back to the upper chest/shoulder level while inhaling.
Keep a stable core and avoid excessive arching of the lower back; maintain scapular control and smooth, controlled reps for the desired number of repetitions.
If using heavy weights, have a spotter or perform a controlled seated dumbbell rack/unrack to avoid strain when starting or finishing the set.
exercises.description: A pressing movement performed lying supine on an incline bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the chest. Dumbbells allow a slightly greater range of motion and independent arm movement compared with a barbell.

exercises.name: Cable Incline Alternative Fly
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and position it between two cable pulleys; select an appropriate light-to-moderate weight on each side.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and position it between two cable pulleys; select an appropriate light-to-moderate weight on each side.
Attach single-handle grips and set the pulleys at or slightly below shoulder height (or low enough so the cables pull upward along an incline line).
Sit and lie back with your head, upper back, and glutes supported on the bench; plant your feet flat on the floor for stability.
Reach up and grasp one handle in each hand with a neutral or palms-facing-in grip; maintain a small bend in the elbows throughout.
Start with your arms open wide so you feel a stretch in the upper chest and shoulders; keep the shoulders down and retracted (no shrugging).
Exhale and bring the hands together in a smooth, arcing motion over your upper chest, squeezing the pectorals at the midpoint without locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly reverse the motion, controlling the cables as you return to the start position until you feel a controlled stretch in the chest.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping torso stable and avoiding bouncing or using momentum; focus on a full range of motion and constant tension.
exercises.description: Performed on an incline bench using dual cable pulleys, the cable incline fly uses a wide arcing motion to bring the handles together and emphasize the upper chest. Maintain a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to isolate the pectorals.

exercises.name: Cable Middle Fly
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to about mid-chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to about mid-chest height and select an appropriate weight.
Stand centered between the towers in a staggered stance (one foot slightly forward) with a slight bend in the knees and a neutral spine.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or palms-facing grip and extend your arms out to the sides so there is tension on the cables; keep a small, constant bend in the elbows.
Retract your shoulder blades and keep the shoulders down; brace your core to prevent torso movement.
Exhale and bring the handles together in front of your chest in a wide arc, leading with the elbows and squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Inhale and slowly reverse the motion with control, allowing the arms to return to the starting position until you feel a gentle stretch in the chest.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, avoiding torso leaning, shoulder shrugging, or locking the elbows.
exercises.description: Standing bilateral cable fly performed with the pulleys set at mid-chest height; the exercise horizontally adducts the arms in a controlled arc to load and contract the pectorals. A staggered stance and slight elbow bend are used to stabilize and protect the joints while the chest performs the work.

exercises.name: Cable Incline Fly
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench between two cable towers (about 30–45° incline).
instructions: Set an incline bench between two cable towers (about 30–45° incline). Attach single D‑handles and set the pulleys to low or just below chest height so the cables travel upward across your chest.
Sit on the bench and lie back so your head rests on the pad; grip one handle in each hand with a neutral or palms‑facing posture and feet flat on the floor.
Retract your shoulder blades and brace your core so your back stays pressed into the bench (inclined supine position). Keep a slight, fixed bend in your elbows throughout the exercise.
Start with your arms out to the sides in line with your chest (open position) and inhale, then exhale as you bring the handles together in a wide arc toward the centerline of your upper chest.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction, focusing on squeezing the pectorals and keeping the elbows slightly bent and stable (avoid driving motion from the wrists or excessive shoulder elevation).
Slowly reverse the movement on the inhale, controlling the cables as you return to the start position until you feel a stretch in the chest without losing scapular retraction.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining core engagement and avoiding excessive momentum or arching of the lower back.
exercises.description: Lying on an incline bench placed between two cable towers, you perform a bilateral fly motion with a slight elbow bend to concentrically bring the handles together and target the upper chest. The movement emphasizes a horizontal adduction arc while the core stabilizes the torso.

exercises.name: Cable Low Fly
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-handle grips; select a moderate weight.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-handle grips; select a moderate weight.
Stand centered between the towers in a staggered or shoulder-width stance with knees soft and core braced (slight forward lean at the hips).
Reach down and grab each handle with a neutral or slightly supinated grip, palms facing in or up, elbows slightly bent and fixed in that position throughout the movement.
Begin with the arms out to the sides and low (handles near hips or knees) and inhale.
Exhale and pull the handles upward and inward in a wide arc, keeping the elbows slightly bent, bringing the hands together or in front of the chest/upper sternum.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction and actively squeeze the pectorals while avoiding excessive shoulder shrugging.
Slowly reverse the motion under control, allowing the cables to return the arms to the start position while maintaining tension in the chest.
Repeat for desired repetitions and re-rack safely; keep torso stable and avoid using momentum.
exercises.description: Standing cable low fly is a low-to-high cable chest fly that targets the pectorals by sweeping the arms upward and together from low pulley positions; performed standing with a slight forward lean and a soft bend in the elbows to emphasize the chest contraction.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Fly
exercises.notes: Step 1: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with palms facing each other and elbows slightly bent.
instructions: Step 1: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with palms facing each other and elbows slightly bent.
Step 2: Brace your core and gently retract and depress your shoulder blades to stabilize the shoulder girdle throughout the movement.

Step 3: Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, keeping the same slight bend in your elbows and stopping when your upper arms are about parallel to the floor or you feel a comfortable stretch in the chest (avoid dropping below bench level).

Step 4: Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulders, then exhale and drive the dumbbells back up in the same arc by contracting the pectorals until the weights meet or are close together over your sternum.
Step 5: Maintain controlled tempo (no bouncing), keep elbows fixed at the same angle, and avoid locking the arms at the top; repeat for desired reps.
exercises.description: Lying flat on a bench while holding dumbbells, the dumbbell fly opens the arms in a wide arc and brings them together to isolate and stretch the chest (pectorals). Movement emphasizes a controlled, arcing motion with a slight bend in the elbows to protect the shoulder joint.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over the midfoot; grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width using an overhand or mixed grip.
Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to set the upper back before the first rep.
Pull the barbell upward toward the lower chest/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and keeping them close to the body; focus on squeezing the upper back at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a flat back, then lower the barbell under control until arms are nearly extended while preserving the hip-hinge position.
Breathe out while pulling the bar up and inhale while lowering it; keep the torso stable and avoid rounding the lower back or using excessive torso momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, and reset your hip-hinge position between sets if you lose posture.
exercises.description: A hip-hinged barbell row that trains the upper back by pulling the loaded barbell toward the torso while maintaining a neutral spine. It also recruits the lats, arms and posterior chain for stability and power.

exercises.name: Cable Wide Grip Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and knee pads so your thighs are secured under the pads when seated.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and knee pads so your thighs are secured under the pads when seated.
Select an appropriate weight and grasp the wide bar with a pronated (overhand) grip wider than shoulder width.
Sit tall with a slight natural arch in the lower back, feet flat, chest up and head neutral.
Begin the rep by inhaling, then exhale as you pull the bar down by driving your elbows down and back toward your ribcage.
Lead the motion with your elbows and squeeze your shoulder blades together at the bottom, bringing the bar to the upper chest or collarbone level.
Pause briefly while maintaining tension in the lats, keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive backward lean or swinging.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to return to the start position with controlled extension of the arms until you feel a full stretch in the lats.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining steady tempo and controlled breathing throughout.
exercises.description: Seated cable wide-grip lat pulldown using an overhand wide bar to pull the cable down toward the upper chest, emphasizing shoulder adduction and scapular depression to work the latissimus dorsi. Performed seated with knees braced to isolate the back and minimize leg drive.

exercises.name: Lever Shrug (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Load the plate-loaded lever machine to the desired weight and stand facing forward with feet hip-width apart, toes pointing straight ahead.
instructions: Load the plate-loaded lever machine to the desired weight and stand facing forward with feet hip-width apart, toes pointing straight ahead.
Grasp the machine handles with a neutral (palms facing the body) grip and let your arms hang fully extended at your sides; maintain a neutral spine and soft knees.
Keep your torso upright and immobile; do not lean or use leg drive throughout the movement.
Exhale and elevate (shrug) your shoulders straight up toward your ears by driving the scapulae upward—think of trying to touch your shoulders to your ears.
Hold the top position and squeeze the traps and levator scapulae for 1–2 seconds while keeping the arms straight and grip firm (forearms engaged).
Inhale and slowly lower the shoulders back down to the starting position under control until the traps are fully stretched.
Repeat for the prescribed repetitions; avoid rolling or rotating the shoulders and avoid excessive neck extension—keep your head neutral and eyes forward.
exercises.description: Standing plate-loaded lever shrugs target the upper trapezius by elevating the shoulders while holding the machine handles; the lift is a vertical scapular elevation with a controlled lowering phase.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; use an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
Hold the bar with arms extended and shoulders protracted, chest up and eyes looking a few feet in front of you to maintain a neutral neck.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows back and up, pulling the bar toward your lower chest / upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Keep the elbows close to the body (not flaring excessively) and avoid using momentum from the hips; breathe out as you pull.
Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position with arms extended while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine; breathe in on the descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, controlling tempo and keeping the torso stable — if you cannot maintain a flat back, reduce weight or stop and reset.
exercises.description: Standing hip-hinged barbell row where you pull a loaded barbell toward the lower chest/upper abdomen; primarily targets the lats and upper back while requiring posterior chain and core stabilization. Maintain a flat back and drive the elbows back to retract the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips until your torso is leaning forward (torso roughly parallel to the floor or about 30–45° above parallel), knees slightly bent; this is a bent-over position — brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip.

Hinge at the hips until your torso is leaning forward (torso roughly parallel to the floor or about 30–45° above parallel), knees slightly bent; this is a bent-over position — brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
Let the dumbbells hang straight down from your shoulders with your arms fully extended; keep your head aligned with your spine.
Initiate the pull by driving your elbows back and up, pulling the dumbbells toward your lower ribs or hip area while squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the top of the movement, maintaining tightness through the lats and upper back without shrugging the shoulders excessively.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position with controlled motion until your arms are extended.
Repeat for the prescribed reps, keeping the torso fixed (avoid using momentum or excessive torso rotation) and continue to breathe: exhale while pulling, inhale while lowering.
exercises.description: A bent-over row performed with dumbbells where you hinge at the hips and pull the weights toward your torso, emphasizing the lats and upper back. The movement also engages the arms and posterior chain to stabilize the hinged position.

exercises.name: Lever One Arm Lateral High Row
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and chest pad so the handle is reachable overhead when seated and your chest is supported against the pad.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and chest pad so the handle is reachable overhead when seated and your chest is supported against the pad.
Select an appropriate weight and sit with feet flat on the floor, chest braced against the pad, and torso upright (no leaning or twisting).
Reach up with one hand and grasp the high lateral handle with a neutral or overhand grip; keep the opposite hand resting or holding a support if available.

Initiate the movement by retracting the shoulder blade and pulling the handle down and slightly back in a controlled arc toward the side of your upper ribcage, keeping the elbow high and out to emphasize the upper back and rear delts.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the peak contraction for 1–2 seconds while maintaining a stable, upright torso (avoid rotating or jerking).
Slowly reverse the motion to return the handle to the start under control, fully extending the arm without collapsing the shoulder.
Perform the desired number of reps for one side, then switch arms and repeat, keeping form consistent on both sides.
exercises.description: Seated one-arm lateral high row performed on a lever machine; you pull a high, lateral handle down and back in an arc to target the upper back and rear shoulder area. The movement emphasizes scapular retraction and the posterior shoulder while using the arm muscles to assist.

exercises.name: Cable Wide Grip Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and knee pads so your thighs are secured under the pads when seated.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and knee pads so your thighs are secured under the pads when seated.
Select an appropriate weight and grasp the wide bar with a pronated (overhand) grip wider than shoulder width.
Sit tall with a slight natural arch in the lower back, feet flat, chest up and head neutral.
Begin the rep by inhaling, then exhale as you pull the bar down by driving your elbows down and back toward your ribcage.
Lead the motion with your elbows and squeeze your shoulder blades together at the bottom, bringing the bar to the upper chest or collarbone level.
Pause briefly while maintaining tension in the lats, keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive backward lean or swinging.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to return to the start position with controlled extension of the arms until you feel a full stretch in the lats.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining steady tempo and controlled breathing throughout.
exercises.description: Seated cable wide-grip lat pulldown using an overhand wide bar to pull the cable down toward the upper chest, emphasizing shoulder adduction and scapular depression to work the latissimus dorsi. Performed seated with knees braced to isolate the back and minimize leg drive.

exercises.name: Cable Straight Arm Pulldown
exercises.notes: Set a high pulley with a straight bar or rope attachment and select a light-to-moderate weight that allows strict form.
instructions: Set a high pulley with a straight bar or rope attachment and select a light-to-moderate weight that allows strict form.

Stand facing the machine about 1–2 feet away, feet shoulder-width apart and knees slightly bent; hinge forward at the hips so your torso is angled forward (standing bent-over position) with a neutral spine.
Reach up and grasp the bar with an overhand grip (palms facing down) about shoulder-width apart; maintain a small, natural bend in the elbows throughout (do not lock).
Brace your core and depress your shoulder blades slightly (avoid shrugging) so tension is felt in the lats before starting the pull.

Exhale and pull the bar down in a controlled arc by extending the shoulders and pulling with the lats until the bar reaches your upper thighs or mid-thigh level; keep the arms long and the elbows fixed in their slight bend.
Squeeze the lats and hold the bottom position briefly while keeping the ribs down and spine neutral.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to rise back to the start position under control, resisting the cable so the lats remain under tension; do not let the lower back round as you return.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining the hip hinge, neutral spine, and long-arm position on every repetition.
exercises.description: Standing cable straight-arm pulldowns hinge from the hips and pull a high cable down with mostly straight arms to load the lats through a long-range extension. The movement emphasizes scapular depression and shoulder extension while keeping the torso braced.

exercises.name: Cable Shrug
exercises.notes: Stand facing the cable machine with a handle attached to a low or mid pulley; feet shoulder-width apart and weight evenly distributed.
instructions: Stand facing the cable machine with a handle attached to a low or mid pulley; feet shoulder-width apart and weight evenly distributed.
Grasp one or two handles with a neutral (palms facing the body) or overhand grip and let your arms hang fully extended at your sides; keep a neutral neck and spine.
Slightly bend the knees and brace your core to stabilize the torso; do not lean forward or backward.
Exhale and elevate (shrug) your shoulders straight up toward your ears, focusing on contracting the trapezius—do not roll or rotate the shoulders.
Hold the peak contraction briefly (about 1 second) while maintaining straight arms and a neutral neck.
Inhale and slowly lower the shoulders back down to the start in a controlled manner until traps are fully stretched.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movement strict and avoiding momentum; reduce weight if you must use arm or torso movement to lift.
exercises.description: Standing cable shrugs target the trapezius by elevating the shoulders against resistance from a cable. Performed with arms straight at the sides, the movement emphasizes a vertical shrug with controlled lowering.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Pendlay Row
exercises.notes: Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; bend at the hips and knees to reach the bar so your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (hip hinge), knees slightly bent.
Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
Brace your core, pull your shoulder blades down and back, then explosively row the bar in a straight line to your lower chest/upper abdomen, driving your elbows back.
Squeeze your upper back at the top for 1 second while keeping the torso stable (no rowing with the hips or excessive torso rotation).
Lower the bar under control all the way back to the floor so it comes to a complete stop, reset any slack, then repeat for the desired reps.
Maintain a flat back and braced core throughout; if your lower back rounds or you must use momentum, reduce weight or raise the bar height.
exercises.description: The Barbell Pendlay Row is a strict horizontal barbell row performed from a dead stop on the floor each rep, emphasizing the upper back and lats while minimizing torso momentum. It uses a hip hinge with a flat back and pulls the bar to the lower chest/upper abdomen before returning it to the floor.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; use an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
Hold the bar with arms extended and shoulders protracted, chest up and eyes looking a few feet in front of you to maintain a neutral neck.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows back and up, pulling the bar toward your lower chest / upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Keep the elbows close to the body (not flaring excessively) and avoid using momentum from the hips; breathe out as you pull.
Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position with arms extended while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine; breathe in on the descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, controlling tempo and keeping the torso stable — if you cannot maintain a flat back, reduce weight or stop and reset.
exercises.description: Standing hip-hinged barbell row where you pull a loaded barbell toward the lower chest/upper abdomen; primarily targets the lats and upper back while requiring posterior chain and core stabilization. Maintain a flat back and drive the elbows back to retract the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Cable seated row
exercises.notes: Lean forward from the hips and reach to grasp the handle with a neutral or overhand grip, arms fully extended and shoulders rounded forward (scapulae protracted).
instructions: Sit on the bench/seat facing the cable machine with feet braced on the footplate and knees slightly bent; select an appropriate weight.
Lean forward from the hips and reach to grasp the handle with a neutral or overhand grip, arms fully extended and shoulders rounded forward (scapulae protracted).
Keep a neutral spine and a slight chest-up posture; initiate the movement by retracting the shoulder blades (pull shoulders down and back) before bending the elbows.

Pull the handle toward your torso (near the lower chest/upper abdomen) driving the elbows back and keeping them close to your sides; squeeze the shoulder blades together at the end of the concentric phase.
Pause briefly at full contraction, maintaining a stable torso and avoiding excessive torso swing or leaning back.
Slowly extend the arms and allow the shoulder blades to protract as you return to the starting position with control, keeping tension on the cable.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining neutral spine, controlled tempo, and full scapular retraction on each rep.
exercises.description: Seated cable row is a horizontal pulling exercise performed from a seated position that primarily targets the latissimus dorsi while working the mid/upper back and arms. It develops scapular retraction and elbow flexion strength through a controlled pull and release.

exercises.name: Cable Bar Lateral Pulldown
exercises.notes: Setup: Select an appropriate weight, sit on the pulldown bench and secure your thighs under the knee pads so your legs are stable; feet flat on the floor.
instructions: Setup: Select an appropriate weight, sit on the pulldown bench and secure your thighs under the knee pads so your legs are stable; feet flat on the floor.
Grip: Reach up and take a wide, overhand grip on the lat pulldown bar (palms facing away) slightly wider than shoulder width.
Start position: Sit tall with a slight natural arch in the lower back, chest lifted, shoulders down and back (scapulae slightly retracted), and arms fully extended overhead holding the bar.
Initiate the pull: Exhale and pull your elbows down and back, driving the bar in a smooth arc toward the upper chest while keeping your torso relatively upright with only a slight backward lean.
Peak contraction: Pause briefly with the bar at or just below the collarbone/upper chest while squeezing the lats and retracting the shoulder blades.
Return: Inhale and slowly allow the bar to rise under control until your arms are fully extended and you feel a stretch in the lats; do not let the weight stack slam.

Form cues & safety: Avoid excessive torso swinging or using momentum, do not pull the bar behind the neck, keep wrists neutral and shoulders from shrugging, and maintain core bracing throughout the set.
Repetitions: Repeat for the desired number of reps with controlled tempo (e.g., 2 seconds down, 1–2 second pause, 2–3 seconds up).
exercises.description: Seated cable bar lateral pulldown that uses a wide overhand grip to pull a bar down toward the upper chest, emphasizing vertical adduction of the humerus to work the latissimus dorsi. The movement also recruits the biceps and upper back muscles to assist the pull.

exercises.name: Cable Wide Grip Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and knee pads so your thighs are secured under the pads when seated.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and knee pads so your thighs are secured under the pads when seated.
Select an appropriate weight and grasp the wide bar with a pronated (overhand) grip wider than shoulder width.
Sit tall with a slight natural arch in the lower back, feet flat, chest up and head neutral.
Begin the rep by inhaling, then exhale as you pull the bar down by driving your elbows down and back toward your ribcage.
Lead the motion with your elbows and squeeze your shoulder blades together at the bottom, bringing the bar to the upper chest or collarbone level.
Pause briefly while maintaining tension in the lats, keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive backward lean or swinging.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to return to the start position with controlled extension of the arms until you feel a full stretch in the lats.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining steady tempo and controlled breathing throughout.
exercises.description: Seated cable wide-grip lat pulldown using an overhand wide bar to pull the cable down toward the upper chest, emphasizing shoulder adduction and scapular depression to work the latissimus dorsi. Performed seated with knees braced to isolate the back and minimize leg drive.

exercises.name: Cable Straight Arm Pulldown
exercises.notes: Set a high pulley with a straight bar or rope attachment and select a light-to-moderate weight that allows strict form.
instructions: Set a high pulley with a straight bar or rope attachment and select a light-to-moderate weight that allows strict form.

Stand facing the machine about 1–2 feet away, feet shoulder-width apart and knees slightly bent; hinge forward at the hips so your torso is angled forward (standing bent-over position) with a neutral spine.
Reach up and grasp the bar with an overhand grip (palms facing down) about shoulder-width apart; maintain a small, natural bend in the elbows throughout (do not lock).
Brace your core and depress your shoulder blades slightly (avoid shrugging) so tension is felt in the lats before starting the pull.

Exhale and pull the bar down in a controlled arc by extending the shoulders and pulling with the lats until the bar reaches your upper thighs or mid-thigh level; keep the arms long and the elbows fixed in their slight bend.
Squeeze the lats and hold the bottom position briefly while keeping the ribs down and spine neutral.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to rise back to the start position under control, resisting the cable so the lats remain under tension; do not let the lower back round as you return.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining the hip hinge, neutral spine, and long-arm position on every repetition.
exercises.description: Standing cable straight-arm pulldowns hinge from the hips and pull a high cable down with mostly straight arms to load the lats through a long-range extension. The movement emphasizes scapular depression and shoulder extension while keeping the torso braced.

exercises.name: Cable Shrug
exercises.notes: Stand facing the cable machine with a handle attached to a low or mid pulley; feet shoulder-width apart and weight evenly distributed.
instructions: Stand facing the cable machine with a handle attached to a low or mid pulley; feet shoulder-width apart and weight evenly distributed.
Grasp one or two handles with a neutral (palms facing the body) or overhand grip and let your arms hang fully extended at your sides; keep a neutral neck and spine.
Slightly bend the knees and brace your core to stabilize the torso; do not lean forward or backward.
Exhale and elevate (shrug) your shoulders straight up toward your ears, focusing on contracting the trapezius—do not roll or rotate the shoulders.
Hold the peak contraction briefly (about 1 second) while maintaining straight arms and a neutral neck.
Inhale and slowly lower the shoulders back down to the start in a controlled manner until traps are fully stretched.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movement strict and avoiding momentum; reduce weight if you must use arm or torso movement to lift.
exercises.description: Standing cable shrugs target the trapezius by elevating the shoulders against resistance from a cable. Performed with arms straight at the sides, the movement emphasizes a vertical shrug with controlled lowering.

exercises.name: Cable Lying Extension Pullover (with rope attachment)
exercises.notes: Setup: Attach a rope handle to the cable and set the pulley to a low or mid-low position in front of the bench.
instructions: Setup: Attach a rope handle to the cable and set the pulley to a low or mid-low position in front of the bench. Position the bench so your head is near the cable column.

Starting position: Lie supine (on your back) on the bench with feet flat on the floor for stability, knees bent if needed. Reach up and grab the rope with both hands (neutral grip), arms extended overhead but with a small bend in the elbows. Maintain a neutral spine.

Body position cues: Keep your ribs down and core braced to avoid excessive lower-back arching; shoulders should be relaxed away from the ears. Hands start above or slightly behind your head depending on pulley placement.

Pulling phase: Exhale and pull the rope in a controlled arc using your lats and shoulder extensors, bringing your hands down and back toward your hips/upper thighs. Keep the elbows slightly bent and fixed in that angle (no large elbow bending).

Peak contraction: Stop when your hands reach the upper-thigh/hip area or you feel a full lat contraction without forcing the shoulder into discomfort. Squeeze the lats briefly at the bottom of the movement.
Return phase: Inhale and slowly reverse the movement, allowing the rope to return overhead under control until you feel a stretch in the lats and chest. Do not let the weight slam the pulley.

Repetitions and safety: Use a weight that allows full range with strict form. Avoid shrugging the shoulders or using momentum; keep movement smooth and controlled. Stop or reduce range if you experience shoulder pain.
exercises.description: Lying on a flat bench with a rope attached to a cable, you extend the shoulders in an arc from an overhead position down toward the hips, emphasizing a lat-driven pullover motion. The rope attachment allows a greater range of motion and a strong contraction of the lats while the chest and triceps assist and the serratus stabilizes the shoulder girdle.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Standing Shoulders Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
Place the barbell in a front-rack position resting on the upper chest/clavicle area with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width and full grip around the bar.
Retract the shoulder blades slightly, keep the chest up and core braced (neutral spine); avoid excessive lumbar arching.
Take a full breath and begin the press by driving the bar upward in a straight line, slightly moving the head back as the bar passes and then returning the head forward under the bar.
Fully extend the elbows at the top with the bar directly over or slightly behind the head, shoulders stacked over hips and feet for balance.
Lower the bar under control back to the front-rack position to complete one rep while inhaling, keeping the elbows tracking slightly forward.
Repeat for desired repetitions, maintaining tight core engagement and controlled tempo; stop or reduce weight if you must excessively lean back to finish reps.
exercises.description: A standing barbell overhead press performed from the front-rack position that primarily develops the delts while requiring the triceps and upper back to assist and stabilize. The lifter presses the barbell from shoulder level to full arm extension overhead while maintaining a braced core.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Overhead Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward or neutral and elbows slightly in front of the body.
Keep the spine neutral and ribcage down to avoid hyperextending the lower back; squeeze the abs throughout.
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled path until the arms are nearly fully extended overhead without locking the elbows.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders stacked over hips and the head neutral (you may slightly move your head back to allow the dumbbells to pass), keeping shoulder blades stable.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting shoulder-height position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding excessive torso lean or lower-back arching; if needed, use a lighter weight to maintain good form.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell overhead press is a compound shoulder exercise where you press dumbbells from shoulder height to full arm extension overhead, primarily working the delts while engaging the triceps and upper back for support and stabilization.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Stand centered between the two towers and grasp each handle with the opposite hand so the cables cross in front of you (or hold same-side handles if preferred).
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (neutral spine). Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral.
Start with the handles low by your thighs; palms can face inward or slightly forward. Maintain a small forward lean from the hips if comfortable to allow a natural arc.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your hands reach approximately shoulder height. Lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts; avoid shrugging the shoulders upward toward your ears.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you begin using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys to isolate the lateral head of the delts; cables provide constant tension while you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The movement is done with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with elbows bent ~90°, palms facing forward or slightly inward and wrists neutral.
Inhale, keep the spine neutral and shoulders down away from the ears.
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled motion until the arms are nearly fully extended overhead (avoid locking the elbows).
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts and keeping the torso stable.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to shoulder height with control to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining a steady tempo and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Use a lighter weight or a spotter if form breaks down or if pressing heavy loads.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell shoulder press is a compound pressing movement performed from a seated, back‑supported position that primarily develops the delts while also engaging the triceps and upper back for stabilization.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Bent Arm Lateral raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides.
instructions: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides.
Bend your elbows to about 90 degrees so the dumbbells are in front of your thighs and palms face down or slightly toward each other.
Keeping the elbows bent and the torso still, raise your forearms and elbows out to the sides until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor (elbows stay higher than the hands).
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining shoulder blade stability; avoid excessive shrugging or leaning the torso.
Slowly lower the weights back to the start under control, keeping tension on the delts and avoiding swinging.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining neutral spine and steady breathing throughout.
exercises.description: A standing lateral raise variation performed with the elbows bent to shorten the lever arm and focus tension on the lateral delts. Lift the dumbbells out to the sides until the upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor, then lower with control.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Delt Fly
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push your butt back and keep a slight bend in the knees until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor; maintain a neutral spine and neck (bent-over position).
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing each other) grip.
Hinge at the hips, push your butt back and keep a slight bend in the knees until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor; maintain a neutral spine and neck (bent-over position).
Let the arms hang straight down from the shoulders with a small bend in the elbows; keep the shoulder blades relaxed but ready to retract.
Exhale and raise the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc (horizontal abduction) leading with the elbows until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor and at shoulder height.
Squeeze the rear delts and pinch the shoulder blades together at the top for a brief pause.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position with control, avoiding momentum or torso swing.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping movement controlled and the torso stable throughout.
exercises.description: A bent-over dumbbell fly that isolates the posterior (rear) deltoids by horizontally abducting the arms from a hinged position. Performed with a hip hinge and slight elbow bend, the movement emphasizes the rear delts while engaging the upper back and traps to stabilize and retract the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Lever Standing Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Adjust the machine weight so you can perform controlled reps without shoulder shrugging.
instructions: Adjust the machine weight so you can perform controlled reps without shoulder shrugging.
Stand upright beside the lever with feet hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and core braced; grasp the handle with the working hand (thumb wrapped) at your side.
Keep a slight bend in the elbow and the wrist neutral; maintain an upright torso with shoulders down and back (no leaning or twisting).
Exhale and lift the arm straight out to the side in a smooth arc until the upper arm reaches roughly shoulder height (parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top while keeping the scapula stable and avoiding excessive shoulder elevation.
Inhale and slowly lower the lever back to the starting position under control, resisting any momentum.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides if performing single-arm sets.
exercises.description: A standing lateral raise performed on a lever/cable station that isolates the lateral head of the deltoid by lifting the arm out to the side from the body. The movement uses a controlled upward arc to shoulder height and a slow descent to emphasize the delts.

exercises.name: Cable One Arm Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on the low pulley and select an appropriate light-to-moderate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on the low pulley and select an appropriate light-to-moderate weight.
Stand upright next to the cable machine with the working side farthest from the pulley, feet shoulder-width apart and knees soft.
Grasp the handle with a neutral grip (thumb slightly up) and let the arm hang at your side with a small bend in the elbow.
Keep your torso tall and core braced; optionally take a small lean away from the machine to increase ROM without rotating the torso.
Exhale and raise the arm laterally away from the body until the hand reaches about shoulder height, leading with the elbow and keeping the wrist neutral.
Pause briefly at the top while avoiding shoulder shrugging or leaning the torso; focus on using the delts to lift.
Inhale and lower the arm slowly and under control back to the start position; repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides.
exercises.description: A single-arm lateral raise performed with a low cable to isolate the lateral deltoid while keeping constant tension. The movement lifts the arm out to the side to about shoulder height with a slight elbow bend.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Supinated Face Pull (with rope)
exercises.notes: Set a cable pulley to about eye to upper-chest height and attach a rope handle.
instructions: Set a cable pulley to about eye to upper-chest height and attach a rope handle.
Stand facing the machine in a staggered or hip-width stance with a slight bend in the knees and a neutral spine.
Reach forward and grab the rope ends with a supinated (palms-up) grip so your thumbs point toward you; step back until there is tension in the cable and your arms are nearly extended.
Brace your core, exhale, and pull the rope toward your face by driving your elbows out to the sides and externally rotating the shoulders so the hands move toward the ears.
At the end of the movement squeeze the shoulder blades together and pause 1–2 seconds while keeping the torso stable (avoid leaning back or shrugging).
Inhale and slowly extend the arms forward to the start position under control, maintaining tension in the cable.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping tempo controlled and focusing on rear delt and upper-back contraction rather than using momentum.
exercises.description: Standing cable face pull with a supinated (palms-up) rope grip emphasizes the rear delts while bringing the shoulder blades together and externally rotating the shoulders. Pull the rope toward your face with elbows high, then return under control.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Standing Shoulders Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
Place the barbell in a front-rack position resting on the upper chest/clavicle area with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width and full grip around the bar.
Retract the shoulder blades slightly, keep the chest up and core braced (neutral spine); avoid excessive lumbar arching.
Take a full breath and begin the press by driving the bar upward in a straight line, slightly moving the head back as the bar passes and then returning the head forward under the bar.
Fully extend the elbows at the top with the bar directly over or slightly behind the head, shoulders stacked over hips and feet for balance.
Lower the bar under control back to the front-rack position to complete one rep while inhaling, keeping the elbows tracking slightly forward.
Repeat for desired repetitions, maintaining tight core engagement and controlled tempo; stop or reduce weight if you must excessively lean back to finish reps.
exercises.description: A standing barbell overhead press performed from the front-rack position that primarily develops the delts while requiring the triceps and upper back to assist and stabilize. The lifter presses the barbell from shoulder level to full arm extension overhead while maintaining a braced core.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Alternate Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip-width apart, knees slightly bent and core (abs) braced to keep the torso stable.
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip-width apart, knees slightly bent and core (abs) braced to keep the torso stable.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward, elbows bent and aligned slightly in front of the body.
Press one dumbbell straight overhead by extending the elbow and driving through the shoulder until the arm is nearly straight (avoid locking the elbow); exhale during the press.
Lower that dumbbell under control back to shoulder height while inhaling.
Immediately press the other dumbbell overhead in the same controlled manner, alternating arms with each repetition.
Keep the ribcage down and avoid excessive arching of the lower back; use the traps to stabilize the shoulders and maintain a neutral head position throughout.
Perform the movement in a slow, controlled rhythm and choose a weight that allows full range of motion without compensatory torso lean.
exercises.description: A standing alternating dumbbell shoulder press where one dumbbell is pressed overhead at a time; primarily develops the delts while the triceps assist and the traps and core stabilize the torso.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing your body) and wrists straight.
Keep a small bend in the elbows throughout the movement and maintain a neutral spine and chest up.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides by lifting at the shoulders, leading with the elbows so the hands finish roughly at shoulder height (arms nearly parallel to the floor).
Avoid shrugging the shoulders up toward the ears; keep the neck long and scapulae stable to minimize excessive trap involvement.
Pause briefly (0–1 second) at the top while maintaining tension in the delts.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and controlled back to the starting position at your sides.
Repeat for desired reps, keeping movement smooth and avoiding momentum or swinging; use lighter weight if form breaks down.
exercises.description: Standing dumbbell lateral raise works the lateral delts by lifting the arms out to the sides to about shoulder height, with a slight elbow bend and controlled motion. It improves shoulder width and isolates the middle shoulder fibers while the traps and forearms assist.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench so the back pad is at a low angle and stand facing the side of the bench.
instructions: Set an incline bench so the back pad is at a low angle and stand facing the side of the bench.
Pick up a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip and take a hip-width stance with a slight bend in the knees.
Hinge forward at the hips and place your forehead or chest gently against the bench pad for support so your torso is close to horizontal (bench-assisted bent-over position).
Let the arms hang straight down from the shoulders with a small bend in the elbows; keep a neutral spine and the head supported on the bench.
Exhale and raise the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc — lead with the elbows — until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor and you feel the rear delts contract.
Pause briefly at the top, then inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and under control back to the starting position.
Keep the movement strict (no torso rotation or momentum) and maintain head/chest support on the bench throughout the set.
Choose a weight that allows controlled form for the full range of motion and perform recommended reps.
exercises.description: A bent-over, bench-supported dumbbell raise that targets the rear (posterior) delts by moving the arms out to the sides while the torso is stabilized. The head/chest is supported on an incline bench to reduce momentum and isolate the rear deltoids.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Alternate Front Raise
exercises.notes: Start standing upright with feet hip‑width apart, knees soft, and a dumbbell in each hand held at your sides (palms facing your thighs).
instructions: Start standing upright with feet hip‑width apart, knees soft, and a dumbbell in each hand held at your sides (palms facing your thighs).
Keep a slight bend in both elbows, shoulders down and back, chest up, and brace your abs to stabilize the torso (neutral spine).
Slowly raise one dumbbell straight in front of you (no swinging or shoulder rotation) until your arm is parallel to the floor and level with the shoulder; exhale as you lift.
Pause briefly at the top, keeping the wrist neutral and the elbow slightly bent, then inhale and slowly lower the weight back to the starting position under control.
Immediately perform the same motion with the opposite arm, alternating sides each repetition while maintaining an upright torso and minimal movement from the hips.
Use a weight you can control for the full range without rocking or using momentum; reduce weight if you find yourself leaning back or swinging.
exercises.description: Standing single‑arm front raises with dumbbells that alternately lift each arm to shoulder height to target the front of the shoulder. Performed slowly and controlled to minimize momentum and maximize deltoid activation.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Supinated Face Pull (with rope)
exercises.notes: Set a cable pulley to about eye to upper-chest height and attach a rope handle.
instructions: Set a cable pulley to about eye to upper-chest height and attach a rope handle.
Stand facing the machine in a staggered or hip-width stance with a slight bend in the knees and a neutral spine.
Reach forward and grab the rope ends with a supinated (palms-up) grip so your thumbs point toward you; step back until there is tension in the cable and your arms are nearly extended.
Brace your core, exhale, and pull the rope toward your face by driving your elbows out to the sides and externally rotating the shoulders so the hands move toward the ears.
At the end of the movement squeeze the shoulder blades together and pause 1–2 seconds while keeping the torso stable (avoid leaning back or shrugging).
Inhale and slowly extend the arms forward to the start position under control, maintaining tension in the cable.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping tempo controlled and focusing on rear delt and upper-back contraction rather than using momentum.
exercises.description: Standing cable face pull with a supinated (palms-up) rope grip emphasizes the rear delts while bringing the shoulder blades together and externally rotating the shoulders. Pull the rope toward your face with elbows high, then return under control.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Stand centered between the two towers and grasp each handle with the opposite hand so the cables cross in front of you (or hold same-side handles if preferred).
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (neutral spine). Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral.
Start with the handles low by your thighs; palms can face inward or slightly forward. Maintain a small forward lean from the hips if comfortable to allow a natural arc.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your hands reach approximately shoulder height. Lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts; avoid shrugging the shoulders upward toward your ears.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you begin using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys to isolate the lateral head of the delts; cables provide constant tension while you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The movement is done with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Standing Shoulders Press
exercises.notes: Light weight, focus on form and range of motion

warmup.notes: Brief warmup to activate target muscles. Can skip if first working set feels warm.
